query: What fish species can be found near the coast of Southern Spain?

treatments.articleTitle: A revision of F. R. Irvine’s Ghanaian marine fishes in the collections of The Natural History Museum, London
treatments.articleAuthor: Edwards, A. J., Gill, A. C. & Abohweyere, P. O.
treatments.chunk_text: LETHRINIDAE One species is found on the coast of West Africa, all others come from the Indo-Pacific region (Carpenter and Allen, 1989).

treatments.articleTitle: A revision of F. R. Irvine’s Ghanaian marine fishes in the collections of The Natural History Museum, London
treatments.articleAuthor: Edwards, A. J., Gill, A. C. & Abohweyere, P. O.
treatments.chunk_text: ECHENEIDAE Seven species may be encountered in the Gulf of Guinea (Lachner and Post in Quéro et al., 1990: 725–728) and one was reported by Irvine from the coast of Ghana.

treatments.articleTitle: A revision of F. R. Irvine’s Ghanaian marine fishes in the collections of The Natural History Museum, London
treatments.articleAuthor: Edwards, A. J., Gill, A. C. & Abohweyere, P. O.
treatments.chunk_text: BELONIDAE Four species may occur off the coast of Ghana (Collette and Parin, 1970), although only one of these (Ablennes hians) was reported by Irvine. The other species which may be expected along the West African mainland coast are Strongylura senegalensis (Valenciennes, 1846), Tylosurus acus rafale Collette and Parin, 1970 and Tylosurus crocodilus crocodilus (Péron and Lesueur, 1821) (Collette and Parin in Quéro et al., 1990: 592–597). On average about 1000 tonnes of needlefishes are caught in the region each year (FAO, 2000).

treatments.articleTitle: A revision of F. R. Irvine’s Ghanaian marine fishes in the collections of The Natural History Museum, London
treatments.articleAuthor: Edwards, A. J., Gill, A. C. & Abohweyere, P. O.
treatments.chunk_text: TRACHINIDAE Six species occur on the mainland coast of the Gulf of Guinea (Roux in Quéro et al., 1990: 893–895) and two were recorded from Ghana by Irvine.

treatments.articleTitle: Afrotropical Asilinae (Asilidae): A provisional key to genera, with a review of the status of Neomochtherus Osten Sacken, 1878, and descriptions of new genera and species
treatments.chunk_text: species from localities quite close to the coast. Apart from being recorded from Lesotho and KwaZulu-Natal, the species is also known from the Eastern Cape, Free State, Mpumalanga and eastern Zimbabwe.

treatments.chunk_text: South Atlantic distribution of this species from 8°S to 18°S. It is presently the only species of Acasta found along the Brazilian coast.

treatments.chunk_text: the Timor Sea. This species is found on both the northwestern and eastern coasts of Queensland, i.e. in the Gulf of Carpentaria and on the Great Barrier Reef.

images.captionText: FIGURE 5. Scrupocellaria curvata Harmer, 1926. A, NHMUK 1928.9. 13.105, part of Holotype of Scrupocellaria curvata. B, NHMUK 1932.9. 16.22, syntype of Scrupocellaria ulrichi Canu & Bassler, 1929. C – F, MTQ G 25340. A, frontal surface of brooding colony. B, frontal surface of branch; note the broken bases of scuta on the inner edge of the opesia. C, frontal surface of brooding colony. D, frontal surface of zooids showing details of scuta and lateral and frontal avicularia. E, frontal surface showing curving frontal avicularia in relation to adjacent other features. F, basal surface at branch bifurcation. Scale bars: 200 µm (A, B); 500 µm (C, E); 100 µm (D, F).

treatments.articleTitle: Nonmarine ostracods (Crustacea) from South Korea, including a description of a new species of Tanycypris Triebel (Cyprididae, Cypricercinae)
treatments.articleAuthor: Chang, Cheon Young, Lee, Jimin & Smith, Robin J.
treatments.chunk_text: found around the Sea of Japan (= the East Sea), the Seto Inland Sea, and the Pacific coast of Honshu and Hokkaido, Japan (Okubo 1980 ; Nakao & Tsukagoshi 2002 ; Smith & Kamiya 2003), as well as Korea (Lee et al. 2000). It is a brackish water species, typically found in estuaries, but it has also been recorded from freshwater rockpools along coasts (Smith & Kamiya 2006).

treatments.chunk_text: species, found in the coast of the Adriatic, Baltic, Barents, Black, Japan, Mediterranean, North-, Norwegian-, Red-, Black- and White Seas (Witkowski et al. 2000, Tsarenko et al. 2009). Black Sea, North-western region (Guslyakov & Nevrova 1998, Guslyakov 2002, Gerasimiuk & Kovtun 2007), Bulgarian coast (Temniskova-Topalova & Petrova-Karadjova 1998), Romanian coast (Bodeanu 1987 –1988, Caraus 2002), Crimea (Proshkina-Lavrenko 1963a, Kucherova 1973, Ryabushko 1991, 2001, Nevrova 1999 a, 2010, Nevrova & Revkov 2003, Nevrova et al. 2003, Senichkina et al. 2004), Caucasian coast (Proshkina-Lavrenko 1963 a, Nevrova 1996, 1999b). response.answer:
The coastal waters of Southern Spain are home to a diverse range of fish species. Here are some common ones:

1. **Mediterranean Sea Bass** (Dicentrarchus labrax): A popular game fish, widely distributed in the Mediterranean.
2. **European Sea Bass** (Dicentrarchus labrax): Similar to the above, but with a more restricted range.
3. **Swordfish** (Xiphias gladius): Common in the eastern Atlantic, including off Southern Spain's coast.
4. **Bluefin Tuna** (Thunnus thynnus): Found in deeper waters, but occasionally caught by recreational anglers.
5. **European Mullet** (Mugil cephalus): Abundant in shallow coastal waters and estuaries.
6. **Dartfish** (Scorpaena scrofa): A common species in rocky areas and reefs.
7. **Gilt-head Bream** (Sparus aurata): Found in sandy and muddy areas, often caught by recreational anglers.
8. **European Flounder** (Platichthys fuscus): Common in shallow waters, including estuaries and coastal bays.
9. **Mediterranean Sea Trout** (Oncorhynchus mykiss): Introduced to the region from North America, now established in some areas.
10. **Groupers** (Epinephelus spp.): Several species of groupers are found off Southern Spain's coast, including the European Grouper (Epinephelus marginatus) and the Moray Eel-like Grouper (Epinephelus morio).
11. **Sardines** (Sardina pilchardus) and **Mackerel** (Scomber scombrus): Abundant in schools, often caught by commercial and recreational fishermen.
12. **Tuna species**: Several tuna species are found off Southern Spain's coast, including the Bluefin Tuna (Thunnus thynnus), Yellowfin Tuna (Thunnus albacares), and Bigeye Tuna (Thunnus obesus).
13. **Dolphins** (Delphinidae spp.) and **Whales** (Balaenoptera spp.): Several species of dolphins and whales can be found in the waters off Southern Spain, including bottlenose dolphins, striped dolphins, and fin whales.

Please note that this is not an exhaustive list, and many other fish species can be found in the coastal waters of Southern Spain. Additionally, some species may be more common or abundant than others depending on factors like location, time of year, and water conditions.
